1: ûḇišnat haʾarbaʿ meʾôṯ wᵉšᵉmônîm: 'In the four hundred and eightieth year' — the chronological anchor for Solomonic history. The 480 years from the Exodus to the fourth year of Solomon (ca. 966 BCE) is structurally significant: 480 = 12 × 40, twelve generations of forty years each — the biblical measure of a complete generational period (Numbers 32:13; Judges 3:11). The number marks the Temple's founding as the culmination of the covenant era that began with the Exodus. The theological point is not a mathematical cipher but a declaration: the Temple is the goal toward which the Exodus covenant was moving.
2: šiššîm ʾammāh ʾorkô wᵉʿeśrîm rōḥab wᵉšᵉlōšîm qômāṯô: '60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, 30 cubits high' — the Temple's dimensions establish the main hall (hêḵāl). A cubit (ʾammāh) is approximately 18 inches (45 cm), making the main hall roughly 90 × 30 × 45 feet. The dimensions are approximately twice those of the Mosaic tabernacle's dimensions (30 × 10 × 10 cubits for the tabernacle proper), preserving the same 3:1 length-to-width ratio. The Temple is an enlargement of the tabernacle, not a replacement; continuity in proportion encodes continuity in theological function.
3: ûlām: 'portico/porch/vestibule' — the frontal entry hall. The three-zone structure of the Temple (ʾûlām, hêḵāl, debîr) mirrors the three-zone structure of the Mosaic tabernacle (outer court, holy place, most holy place) and the graduated approach from public to private to most intimate. The ʾûlām's width equaled the full width of the Temple (20 cubits) and projected 10 cubits forward, forming the visible public face of the building.
4: ḥallônê šᵉqûpîm ʾăṭûmîm: 'windows of fixed lattice-work' — the Temple had recessed lattice windows that admitted light while maintaining privacy. The term šᵉqûpîm (from šāqap, to look out) appears elsewhere for peering through (Genesis 26:8; Judges 5:28), while ʾăṭûmîm can mean 'stopped' or 'framed.' These architecturally distinctive openings mark the threshold between ordinary and sacred space.
5: yāṣîaʿ: 'side chamber/annex' — the three-story side-rooms surrounding the main building on three sides (all except the front). These chambers were attached to the outer wall without penetrating it (v6): no breach was made in the Temple's primary walls. The side chambers provided storage and perhaps priestly quarters while insulating the sacred core.
6: migraʿôṯ: 'offsets/ledges' — the Temple's outer wall was built in three steps, each story's floor resting on a ledge (rebate) in the wall. This eliminated the need to embed beams into the sacred wall itself (v7), maintaining the wall's integrity. The architectural detail is noted to explain how the annex attached without structural violation of the Temple's primary enclosure.
7: ʾăḇānîm šᵉlēmôṯ massāʿ nibᵉneh: 'complete/finished stones from the quarry it was built' — the stones were dressed before arriving at the Temple Mount. Šālem (complete, perfect) also carries the sense of wholeness or peace (šālôm). No hammer (maqāḇôṯ), axe (gārzēn), or iron tool (kᵉlî barzel) was heard at the building site. The silence is a sacred threshold marker: the work of fashioning is completed elsewhere; at the sacred site only assembly occurs. Iron implements signify warfare and violence in OT symbology (cf. the altar's uncut stones, Exodus 20:25; Deuteronomy 27:5) — the house of the God of peace is built in silence and without weapons.
8: petaḥ haṣṣēlāʿ: 'the entrance of the middle floor/side-chambers' — the architectural access point to the three levels of side rooms. The side chambers were accessed from the right (south) side of the Temple via a staircase arrangement going up from floor to floor, confirming the three-story arrangement described in vv5-6.
9: wayyibᵉn ʾeṯ-habbayiṯ wayyᵉkallēhû: 'He built the house and completed it' — verse 9 reports the completion of the main structure's walls and roof (cedar beams and planks). Kālāh (to complete, finish) is used here in a partial sense — the outer structure is complete before the interior work begins (vv14ff). The cedar roof capped the stone walls, completing the primary enclosure.
10: hayyāṣîaʿ… ʿeśer bāʾammāh gōḇhô: 'the side-chambers…five cubits high each' — the verse confirms the annex dimensions (5 cubits per story, three stories = 15 cubits total height) and that the cedar beams of the annex attached to the main Temple walls. The structural repetition underscores the care taken to document the Temple's construction accurately from official building accounts.
11: wayᵉhî dᵉḇar YHWH ʾel-šᵉlōmōh: 'And the word of YHWH came to Solomon' — mid-construction, YHWH interrupts with a conditional promise. The word (dāḇār) of YHWH intrudes into the building project to assert the theological condition governing the Temple's efficacy. The timing is significant: not before construction begins (which would make the promise appear unconditional) and not after completion (too late to function as warning). The word comes during the work, reshaping the meaning of what is being built.
12: ʾim-tēlēk bᵉḥuqqōṯay: 'If you walk in my statutes' — the Davidic covenant's temple-provision is conditional on obedience. While the eternal dynasty was unconditionally promised to David (2 Samuel 7:14-16), the Temple's ongoing function as YHWH's dwelling depends on Solomon's (and his successors') faithfulness. Ḥuqqôṯ (statutes), mišpāṭîm (ordinances), miṣwôṯ (commandments) — the triad of covenant obligation terminology makes clear that the Temple does not replace the covenant's behavioral expectations. The later destruction of the Temple (2 Kgs 25) is the fulfillment of this condition's violation.
13: wᵉšāḵantî bᵉṯôḵ bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿᵉzōḇ ʾeṯ-ʿammî yiśrāʾēl: 'I will dwell in the midst of the children of Israel and I will not abandon my people Israel' — the Temple's purpose stated: not to house a deity (ancient Near Eastern temples were divine palaces where the god was fed) but to provide the dwelling-point of YHWH's presence among his people. The negation wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿᵉzōḇ (I will not abandon/forsake) uses the covenantal ʿāzaḇ — the same verb in Deuteronomy 31:6, Joshua 1:5, and Hebrews 13:5. The promise is relational, not architectural: the Temple exists to facilitate divine-human dwelling, not to confine God (cf. 1 Kings 8:27).
14: wayyibᵉn šᵉlōmōh ʾeṯ-habbayiṯ wayyᵉkallēhû: 'So Solomon built the house and completed it' — verse 14 repeats the completion formula from v9, now framing the summary after the divine interlude of vv11-13. The repetition marks a structural boundary: vv1-13 = construction overview and divine word; vv14ff = interior furnishing details. The narrative moves from the external shell to the interior treatment (cedar paneling, olive wood carvings, gold overlay).
15: bᵉḵiṯlôṯ ḥāllôṯ ʿaṣê ʾārāzîm: 'boards of cedar wood' — the interior was completely paneled with cedar (ʾerez, the prized cedar of Lebanon), covering all stone walls from floor to ceiling. The cedar paneling concealed all the stone, making the interior entirely an organic wooden structure overlaid with gold. No stone was visible within the sacred space — a deliberate contrast with the stone altars and monuments of Canaanite worship.
16: wayyibᵉn ʿeśrîm ʾammāh miyrᵉḵeṯê habbayiṯ bᵉṣalʿôṯ ʾarāzîm: 'He built twenty cubits from the rear of the house with cedar planks' — the rear 20 cubits of the Temple were floored and walled with cedar from floor to ceiling, creating the separate inner sanctuary (debîr). The cedar floor-to-ceiling partition enclosed the Holy of Holies as a completely wood-paneled chamber within the larger building. The 20-cubit demarcation turns the rear third of the Temple's length into the most sacred zone.
17: wᵉhabbayiṯ hûʾ hêḵāl lifᵉnāyw ʾarbaʿîm bāʾammāh: 'And the house — the main hall before it — forty cubits' — verse 17 clarifies the spatial division: the main hall (hêḵāl) = 40 cubits; the inner sanctuary (debîr) = 20 cubits; together = 60 cubits (the full length established in v2). The hêḵāl (main hall/nave) derives from the Akkadian ekallu (palace), reflecting the ancient Near Eastern convention that a god's temple was the god's palace.
18: ʾereẓ ʾel-habbayiṯ penîmāh: 'cedar toward the inside of the house' — the cedar was carved with gourds (pᵉqāʿîm) and open flowers (pᵉṭûrê ṣîṣîm). The botanical imagery on every interior surface was intentional: the Temple's interior recreated the garden of Eden — the sacred space where God and humans had walked together before the fall. The gourd motif appears also on the bronze Sea (1 Kings 7:24) and on the high priest's robe (Exodus 28:33).
19: wᵉhaddebîr bᵉṯôḵ habbayiṯ mippᵉnîmāh hēkîn: 'He prepared the inner sanctuary in the innermost part of the house' — debîr (the inner sanctuary) is the term for the Holy of Holies. Its etymology is disputed: possibly from dāḇār (word/oracle) — the place where YHWH speaks; or from an Ugaritic root meaning 'back room.' The LXX renders it to hagion ton hagion (holy of holies). Its purpose is stated: to receive the ark of the covenant — it is the ark's permanent throne room.
20: wᵉhaddebîr ʿeśrîm ʾammāh ʾorkô wᵉʿeśrîm ʾammāh rōḥab wᵉʿeśrîm ʾammāh qômāṯô: '20 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, 20 cubits high' — the Holy of Holies is a perfect cube. A cube is the only shape where all dimensions are equal — it represents divine perfection and completeness. The New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:16) is also a perfect cube (12,000 stadia on each side), signaling that it is the eschatological fulfillment of what the Holy of Holies anticipated: the cosmic dwelling of God with his people where the cube-shape expands to fill all creation. Gold overlay (zāhāb sāgûr, refined/pure gold) covered every surface.
21: wayyᵉṣap šᵉlōmōh ʾeṯ-habbayiṯ mippᵉnîmāh zāhāḇ sāgûr: 'Solomon overlaid the interior of the house with pure gold' — sāgûr (refined/locked/pure) gold is the highest grade, used for the most sacred furnishings (the lampstand, Exodus 25:31; the mercy seat, Exodus 25:17). Cedar chains were drawn across the front of the Holy of Holies as symbolic barriers before the veil. Every visible interior surface was gold — anticipating the divine kāḇôd (glory) that would fill the completed building (1 Kings 8:10-11).
22: wᵉḵol-habbayiṯ ṣippāh zāhāḇ: 'The whole house he overlaid with gold' — v22 summarizes the gold overlay covering the entire interior, including the altar belonging to the inner sanctuary (hammizḇēaḥ ʾăšer laddebîr). This cedar altar, distinct from the bronze altar in the outer court, stood before the entrance to the Holy of Holies overlaid with gold. The completeness of the gold overlay makes the entire interior a luminous golden space.
23: wayyas ̣ baddebîr šᵉnê kᵉrûḇîm ʿaṣê šāmen: 'In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim of olive wood' — kᵉrûḇîm (cherubim) are the winged divine attendants who guard the divine presence (Genesis 3:24: cherubim guard Eden's entrance; Exodus 25:18-20: cherubim on the mercy seat). ʿAṣê šāmen is olive wood — the oiliest, densest wood available in the region, associated with blessing and anointing. Each cherub was 10 cubits (15 feet) tall — enormous guardians in the Temple's most sacred space.
24: ḥāmēš ʾammôṯ kᵉnaṣ hakkᵉrûḇ hāʾeḥāḏ: 'Five cubits was one wing of the cherub' — each cherub had a wingspan of 10 cubits (two wings × 5 cubits each). The two cherubim standing side by side spanned the full 20-cubit width of the Holy of Holies. Each figure was 10 cubits tall with a 10-cubit wingspan — the same dimension for height and wingspan, creating a proportionally balanced guardian and filling the Holy of Holies from wall to wall.
25: wᵉhakkᵉrûḇ haššēnî ʿeśer bāʾammāh: 'The second cherub was ten cubits' — v25 confirms that both cherubim were identical (same dimensions, same measure, same form). The precision of the description — twice stated — ensures the symmetry of divine honor: neither cherub was greater. The parallel dimensions reflect the literary concern for sacred exactness that marks the entire Temple-building narrative and echoes Exodus 25-31.
26: qômāṯ hakkᵉrûḇ hāʾeḥāḏ ʿeśer bāʾammāh ûḵēn hakkᵉrûḇ haššēnî: 'The height of one cherub was ten cubits, and likewise the second cherub' — the redundant confirmation of equal height is typical of the Temple narrative's precision. The cherubim's 10-cubit height made them approximately 15 feet tall — they dominated the 20-cubit-high (30-foot) chamber. The visual effect: golden walls, golden cherubim 15 feet tall with 30-foot combined wingspan, and the ark beneath their outstretched wings.
27: wayyittēn ʾeṯ-hakkᵉrûḇîm bᵉṯôḵ habbayiṯ happᵉnîmî: 'He placed the cherubim in the inner house' — the cherubim's wing-tips touched the opposing walls (each outer wing) and each other (each inner wing), creating a continuous 20-cubit canopy from wall to wall. The ark sat beneath the intersection of their inner wings (1 Kings 8:6-7). The cherubim formed the living throne of YHWH — the Temple architecture embodied YHWH yōšēḇ hakkᵉrûḇîm (who sits enthroned upon the cherubim; 1 Samuel 4:4; Psalms 80:1).
28: wayyᵉṣap šᵉlōmōh ʾeṯ-hakkᵉrûḇîm zāhāḇ: 'Solomon overlaid the cherubim with gold' — the olive-wood cherubim were covered with gold, as were all surfaces of the inner sanctuary. This gold-on-olive-wood composite combines the practical (structural olive wood) with the symbolic (gold overlay for sacred representation). The olive wood beneath the gold may carry a secondary association: olive oil is the medium of anointing (māšîaḥ, anointed one) — the cherubim are made of wood associated with consecration, then consecrated further with gold overlay.
29: wᵉʾēṯ kol-qîrôṯ habbayiṯ mᵉsaḇîḇ qallāʿ pᵉtûḥôṯ tîmōrōṯ ûpᵉṭûrê ṣîṣîm: 'On all the walls of the house around, both inner and outer rooms, he carved engravings of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers' — the three elements of the carved program create an Eden environment: cherubim (the divine guardians of God's garden-presence, Genesis 3:24), palm trees (tîmōrôṯ, the tall graceful trees of the promised land's abundance), and open flowers (pᵉṭûrê ṣîṣîm, blossoming life). The Temple's interior decoration deliberately recreates the garden of Eden — the primordial sacred space where divine and human presence cohabited — framing every act of worship within the narrative of paradise lost and the hope of paradise restored.
30: wᵉʾeṯ-qarkāʿ habbayiṯ ṣippāh zāhāḇ: 'The floor of the house he overlaid with gold' — even the floor received gold overlay, completing the all-gold interior. The effect: standing inside the Temple, every surface in every direction — above, below, left, right — was gold. The space was designed to overwhelm the senses with the visual language of divine glory.
31: wᵉʾeṯ-petaḥ haddebîr ʿāśāh daltôṯ ʿaṣê šāmen: 'For the entrance of the inner sanctuary he made doors of olive wood' — the debîr's entrance had a five-sided door frame (mezûzāh, ḥamēšîṯ). The door construction — olive wood (same as the cherubim) with carved cherubim, palms, and flowers overlaid with hammered gold — maintained the Eden imagery to the very threshold of the most sacred space. The five-sided (pentagonal) frame is unusual and may reflect a distinctive architectural feature of the inner sanctuary's entrance.
32: wᵉqālaʿ ʿălêhem kᵉrûḇîm wᵉtîmōrōṯ ûpᵉṭûrê ṣîṣîm: 'He carved on them cherubim, palms, and open flowers, and overlaid them with gold' — the two olive-wood doors of the inner sanctuary carried the full carved program of the interior walls: cherubim, palms, and flowers. The doors were the first point of visual encounter for those approaching the Holy of Holies — the carved program announced the Eden-sanctuary before the viewer entered.
33: wᵉḵēn ʿāśāh lᵉpetaḥ hahêḵāl mezûzôṯ ʿaṣê šāmen rᵉḇîʿîṯ: 'So also he made for the entrance of the main hall door-frames of olive wood — a fourth part' — the main hall's entrance had a four-sided (rectangular) frame, as opposed to the inner sanctuary's five-sided frame. The differentiated frame shapes (five-sided for the debir, four-sided for the hekhal) may signal the graduated sanctity of the two spaces.
34: ûšᵉtê daltôṯ ʿaṣê bᵉrôšîm: 'The two doors were of cypress wood' — the main hall's doors were cypress (bᵉrôšîm) rather than olive wood; the inner sanctuary's doors were olive wood. The use of different woods for the two doors may encode a distinction in sanctity, or reflect material availability. The two-leaf arrangement of each door (two halves, each half folding) created a flexible entry system for the Temple's regular priestly use.
35: wᵉqālaʿ kᵉrûḇîm wᵉtîmōrōṯ ûpᵉṭûrê ṣîṣîm ûmᵉṣuppeh zāhāḇ mûšāh ʿal-hamᵉḥuqqeh: 'He carved cherubim, palms, and open flowers, and overlaid them with gold fitted on the carved work' — the gold was not simply applied as sheets but hammered to fit the relief carvings of the cypress doors. This goldsmithing technique (mûšāh ʿal-hamᵉḥuqqeh, gold set/fitted over the carved relief) shows a high level of craft: the carved decoration was preserved in three dimensions beneath the gold sheathing.
36: wayyibᵉn ʾeṯ-heḥāṣēr happᵉnîmîṯ: 'He built the inner court with three courses of dressed stone and one course of cedar beams' — the inner court surrounding the Temple proper used alternating courses of stone and cedar in a ratio of 1 Kings 3:1. This construction method reflects Solomonic building practice learned from Phoenician craftsmen. The inner court (ḥāṣēr happᵉnîmîṯ) corresponds to the tabernacle's courtyard enclosing the sacred precinct.
37: bišnaṯ hārbîʿîṯ yussad bêṯ YHWH bᵉyeraḥ ziw: 'In the fourth year the house of YHWH was founded, in the month of Ziv' — the construction record gives both the starting date (v37) and the completion date (v38) with month names. Ziv (the second month) is the Canaanite name for the month; the post-exilic designation is Iyyar. The specific month naming grounds the Temple's construction in historical time — not mythological time — a feature that distinguishes Israelite historiography from neighboring ANE building accounts which typically locate temple constructions in cosmic rather than historical time.
38: ûbiššᵉnāh hāʾaḥaṯ ʿeśrēh bᵉyeraḥ bûl hûʾ haḥōdeš hašᵉmînî kālāh habbayiṯ lᵉḵol-dᵉḇārāyw wᵉḵol-mišpāṭāyw wayyibᵉnēhû šeḇaʿ šānîm: 'In the eleventh year, in the month of Bul — the eighth month — the house was completed in every detail according to all its specifications. He built it in seven years.' The completion in seven years is the structural signature of the narrative: seven years = the number of divine completion (Genesis 2:1-3, the seventh day of creation rest). The Temple built in the number of completion consecrates it as the earthly counterpart of YHWH's cosmic dwelling established at creation. Bul (the eighth month, post-exilic: Ḥeshvan) grounds the completion in the agricultural year's early rainy season.
